history.note: Added open backlog recommendations for the remaining implementation plan items with scoped criteria and priority.

Added an Obsidian-backed note workflow recommendation for token-efficient context management.
Added a recommendation to delegate prior-art and workflow surveys to subagents before introducing new skills, MCP servers, rules, or workflows.

closeout_criteria: Runtime evidence shows every intended hook event reached the router during verification.
Any coverage gaps or policy mismatches are documented with a fix or explicit follow-up recommendation.
The hook routing notes or references are updated so the verified behavior is easy to re-check later.
Hermes config checks still pass after the verification work.
evidence: Runtime-verified /Users/bernardchase/.hermes/agent-hooks/hook_router.py with representative pre_tool_call, pre_llm_call, pre_approval_request, post_tool_call, and on_session_start events; each invocation returned success and reached the router.
Confirmed the hook router blocks destructive terminal commands during pre_tool_call and that hooks_auto_accept remains true in config.yaml.
Updated the routing notes in capability-audit-summary.md so the verified behavior is easy to re-check later.
execution_criteria: Enumerate the intended hook surfaces in config.yaml and confirm each one is routed through /Users/bernardchase/.hermes/agent-hooks/hook_router.py.
Exercise a representative sample of hook events and observe them reaching the centralized router instead of ad hoc logic.
Confirm hooks_auto_accept behaves as intended for the sampled event set and document any exceptions.
Keep hook dispatch logic single-sourced; do not introduce parallel hook handling paths while verifying coverage.

history.note: Seeded from the remaining implementation plan.

notes: Derived from the capability audit priority list.

closeout_criteria: A declarative registry exists and is referenced from the backlog/orchestrator documentation.
The weekly review cadence can be followed without guessing which script or doc to use.
A stale-item review path exists and clearly explains what evidence is needed to keep, defer, or drop an item.
At least one registry entry can be traced from backlog item to workflow reference and back.
evidence: Canonicalized the workflow registry with an explicit next-action column so review paths are discoverable from the registry alone.
Updated the weekly and stale-item playbooks plus backlog README to point back to the registry as the canonical cadence index.
Documented the review entry points so the backlog bridge helpers are visible without relying on prose-only instructions.
execution_criteria: Define the canonical workflow registry entries for core Hermes maintenance and operating routines.
Wire the weekly backlog review and stale-item review entry points into the documented workflow path.
Ensure the workflow registry points to the backlog bridge helpers rather than duplicating logic in prose-only docs.
Make it possible to identify the next review action for a workflow item from the registry alone.

history.note: Seeded from the remaining implementation plan.
Canonicalized workflow registry and review cadence references so the next action is discoverable from the registry.

notes: This item is about making operational routines explicit and durable, not adding new execution logic.
items.summary: Create a durable registry for Hermes workflows and make weekly and stale-item reviews a first-class, repeatable operating path.
items.title: Canonicalize the workflow registry and review cadence

closeout_criteria: A sample execution produces telemetry that can be inspected later.
A backlog or review workflow can consume that telemetry to guide a concrete recommendation.
The telemetry docs explain what gets stored, what stays out, and how the data feeds improvement.
No sensitive data is captured in the durable telemetry path.
execution_criteria: Capture the key execution metadata needed to compare actual outcomes against expected outcomes for backlog or workflow work.
Write or surface telemetry in a durable location that can be read during backlog review without manual reconstruction.
Use at least one telemetry signal to influence a triage or prioritization decision.
Keep the telemetry output free of secrets, tokens, or other sensitive material.

history.note: Seeded from the remaining implementation plan.

notes: This is the operational follow-through for the agent-self-improvement telemetry work already started.
Focus on durable decision support, not raw logging volume.
items.summary: Turn execution telemetry into a durable review signal so Hermes can use observed behavior to improve prioritization and skill selection.
items.title: Complete the telemetry and decision-memory loop

closeout_criteria: A clear lifecycle path exists for skill discovery, maintenance, and archival decisions.
At least one overlap/prune decision is recorded with rationale and evidence.
Curator guidance points to the same lifecycle rules used in backlog review.
The workflow can be repeated without relying on tribal knowledge.
execution_criteria: Define how a skill is discovered, promoted, maintained, or retired within the Hermes workflow.
Identify at least one overlapping or duplicated skill area and document the rule used to keep, merge, or archive it.
Align curator guidance with the backlog and telemetry signals so maintenance decisions are repeatable.
Make the skill lifecycle easy to inspect from the project docs without chasing hidden conventions.

notes: Use telemetry and backlog review output to justify promotion or retirement decisions.
Prefer cleanup and consolidation over new skill proliferation.
items.summary: Make skill promotion, overlap resolution, and archival decisions explicit so the Hermes skill set stays lean and discoverable.
items.title: Finalize skills lifecycle and curator alignment

closeout_criteria: The MCP guidance is discoverable from the main Hermes workflow documentation.
A representative workflow can be executed or reasoned through using the documented MCP path.
The guidance includes both the preferred route and the fallback route when the preferred MCP path is unavailable.
The references are stable enough to reuse in future capability reviews.
execution_criteria: Document which MCP servers and tool families Hermes should prefer for common workflow classes.
Provide at least one explicit wrapper or guidance path for a representative external integration use case.
Verify that the chosen MCP route can be followed end-to-end without inventing an unlisted fallback.
Document fallback behavior for unavailable or failing MCP tools.

notes: Keep this item about routing and integration guidance, not about building new MCP infrastructure from scratch.
Use it to reduce guesswork when choosing between local, web, browser, or MCP-backed paths.

items.title: Add MCP workflow guidance and wrappers for external integration

closeout_criteria: A budget/eval/rollback policy exists in durable Hermes docs or automation.
At least one sample change path demonstrates how the gates are applied.
The rollback conditions are specific enough that a reviewer can decide without guessing.
The policy integrates with the weekly review or stale-item review cadence.
execution_criteria: Define practical budget thresholds or gating rules for risky workflow, skill, or hook changes.
Specify the evaluation checks that must pass before a change can be promoted.
Document rollback or abort criteria that can be triggered when a change regresses behavior.
Make the criteria usable from the same backlog and review loop that governs the rest of Hermes.

notes: Lower priority than the core orchestration path, but important for keeping changes safe as Hermes grows.
Favor simple, observable gates over elaborate policy machinery.
items.summary: Define the change-control criteria Hermes should use before promoting risky capability updates, and make rollback conditions explicit.
items.title: Add budgets, evals, and rollback gates for risky changes
Document updater stash conflict-resolution options
items.summary: Capture high-priority guidance for resolving Hermes updater stash conflicts so local changes and upstream updates can be reconciled without leaving the user stuck.
execution_criteria: Document the supported recovery paths when hermes update stashes local changes and restore conflicts occur.
Include a safe decision tree for choosing between applying the stash, resolving conflicts in place, or restarting from a clean worktree.
Record the verification steps needed after each recovery path so the update can be trusted.
Call out the known conflict files and the preserved stash reference from the recent updater run so the guidance is grounded in a real incident.
closeout_criteria: The updater guidance lists concrete resolution options with when-to-use notes.
The guidance includes at least one verified recovery procedure that can be repeated later.
The preserved stash reference and conflicted paths are recorded for traceability.
The backlog item links to the updater guidance or runbook entry that operators can follow during future update conflicts.
evidence: Verified the clean-worktree fallback in a detached worktree during closeout.
notes: High-priority recommendation based on a real hermes update conflict where local changes could not be restored cleanly.
Prefer a user-facing decision tree over ad hoc recovery instructions.

history.note: Added from the updater stash conflict incident as a high-priority backlog recommendation.
Documented the updater stash recovery decision tree and verified the clean-worktree fallback in a detached worktree.

items.title: Use Obsidian notes as a token-efficient Hermes context layer
items.summary: Define a durable workflow where Hermes uses concise Obsidian notes as an external project memory layer to reduce context load while keeping long-lived project state easy to retrieve.
execution_criteria: Delegate the public skill/workflow survey across Hermes, Codex, Claude Code, Clawdhub, and related sources to one or more subagents before deciding to design the note layer from scratch.
Have the subagents report back a concise synthesis of existing patterns, gaps, and reusable ideas, then use that synthesis to decide whether to adapt an existing workflow or create a new one.
Document a note structure for project summaries, decisions, open questions, and durable references that Hermes can load selectively.
Define a retrieval policy that keeps Hermes from loading the full vault and instead reads only the index note plus the notes relevant to the current task.
Show how session-level compression and persistent memory interact with the note workflow so token usage stays predictable.
Include a repeatable update path for writing back concise session summaries or decisions after major work.
Keep the workflow compatible with the existing Hermes docs and note-taking conventions.
closeout_criteria: The workflow is documented in a reusable note or runbook that explains when Hermes should read notes, compress context, or rely on built-in memory.
A minimal Obsidian note template or folder convention exists for project indexes and task summaries.
At least one verified example shows Hermes using the note layer to answer or continue work without loading unnecessary context.

The guidance explicitly records which existing Hermes/Codex/Claude Code/Clawdhub-style skills were reviewed, which subagents performed the survey, and whether the workflow reuses or adapts them instead of reinventing the pattern.
The guidance makes the limits explicit so the vault is an external knowledge base, not a transparent automatic memory store.
evidence: Survey synthesis from parallel subagents: public prior-art scan, Obsidian context-layer pattern scan, and local RAG routing scan.
notes: Lower priority than the open P0 updater conflict item because this is a workflow optimization, not a recovery blocker.
Use concise summaries and linked notes rather than trying to make Obsidian act like transparent agent memory.

history.note: Added as a workflow recommendation for Obsidian-backed token-efficient context management.
Documented the Obsidian-backed context layer, local RAG profile shape, and selective retrieval smoke-test example after the survey gate findings.

items.title: Implement subagent-led prior-art surveys before creating new capabilities
items.summary: Create a reusable Hermes workflow that delegates public skill, MCP server, rule, and workflow surveys to subagents before introducing any new skill, MCP server, rule, or workflow from scratch.
execution_criteria: Define a standard survey brief that instructs subagents to search public Hermes, Codex, Claude Code, Clawdhub, and related docs before any new capability is designed.
Specify the subagent roles, output schema, and citation format for reporting existing patterns, gaps, and reusable techniques.
Implement a reusable workflow or skill that fans the survey out to multiple subagents and aggregates their findings into a concise synthesis.
Require the synthesis to recommend reuse, adaptation, or greenfield implementation, with reasons.
Integrate the survey step into the intake path for new skills, MCP servers, rules, and workflows so the check happens before implementation starts.
closeout_criteria: The subagent survey workflow is documented and reusable from the Hermes operating docs or a dedicated skill.
A completed example shows multiple subagents performing the survey and a synthesized recommendation being produced from their outputs.
The workflow records which sources were checked and which existing patterns were reused or intentionally not reused.
The intake path for new capabilities clearly includes the survey gate before creation work begins.
The process stays lightweight enough to use routinely without forcing a full manual literature review every time.
evidence: Survey synthesis from three parallel subagents covering Hermes, Claude Code, Codex, and public prior-art examples.
notes: This is a process-gating recommendation, not a feature implementation request.
Prefer subagents for broad survey work so the primary agent stays focused on synthesis and decision-making.

history.note: Added as a reusable gate to delegate prior-art surveys to subagents before introducing new capabilities.
Documented a reusable survey-first intake gate with lane-based subagent prompts, required output shape, and backlog integration guidance.

items.title: Operationalize a default local RAG profile for Hermes
items.summary: Create a reusable local retrieval workflow that combines MCP-backed retrieval sources, selective Obsidian notes, and the survey-first intake gate so Hermes can answer with local context without loading the full vault.
execution_criteria: Define the canonical retrieval sources and their roles, including selective Obsidian index notes, an MCP-backed local retrieval engine such as qmd or an equivalent, and the existing workflow registry.
Specify routing rules for when Hermes should query notes, MCP tools, or existing skills, and when it should fall back rather than inventing hidden retrieval paths.
Document a minimal profile or runbook that enables the local RAG workflow without turning the vault into transparent agent memory.
Integrate the survey-first gate so any new retrieval skill, MCP server, rule, or workflow is researched by subagents before being added.
Verify with at least one concrete example that a task can continue from selective local retrieval instead of broad context loading.
closeout_criteria: A reusable runbook or profile documents source selection, routing rules, and fallback behavior for the local RAG workflow.
A verified example or smoke test shows Hermes using selective local retrieval from notes and/or MCP sources successfully.
The survey gate and workflow registry clearly point to the intake path for future retrieval-related capabilities.
The limits are explicit: no full-vault loading, no transparent memory, and no undocumented retrieval fallback.
notes: This recommendation follows the current research finding that Hermes already has native MCP and note-layer scaffolding, but not a single default local RAG workflow.
Prefer an explicit retrieval profile and documented source-selection rules over broad automatic context ingestion.

history.note: Added a backlog recommendation for a default local RAG profile with selective retrieval, MCP routing, and survey-first intake.
Documented a reusable local RAG profile/runbook with explicit routing rules, no hidden fallback, and a selective-retrieval smoke test.
